drafts.title: pi-astack is a personal harness-specific workflow repo, not a distribution
drafts.compiledTruth: pi-astack is a personal workflow repository for a specific harness, modeled after garrytan/gstack's relationship to claude-code. It is not a Linux distribution, npm package collection, or multi-harness adapter. It serves one author (alfadb) using one harness (pi), co-locating all workflow resources (extensions, skills, prompts) in a single monorepo. The repository is not optimized for external contributors or cross-harness entry points — its value is focused integration, not generality.

drafts.title: Retired vendor references plus owned port layer architecture
drafts.compiledTruth: The repository follows an owned port layer architecture with retired vendor references available on demand. There are currently no active vendor submodules; historical upstreams such as vendor/gstack/ are recorded in UPSTREAM.md with their last pinned SHA and should be cloned temporarily outside the tracked repo when diff/reference reading is needed. The owned port layer (extensions/, skills/, prompts/) contains alfadb's adaptations and original work. The port layer never imports or sources from vendor/ paths.

drafts.title: Mount as git submodule, load via local path, edit in place
drafts.compiledTruth: pi-astack must be mounted as a git submodule at ~/.pi/agent/skills/pi-astack/. pi loads it via a local path in settings.json (packages: ["~/.pi/agent/skills/pi-astack"]), not a git: URL. The author can cd into the submodule at any time to edit directly; changes take effect immediately via /reload. All commits happen inside the pi-astack submodule; ~/.pi only tracks the submodule pointer SHA. This collapses the author-is-user-is-developer loop into a single working directory.
drafts.sourceHeading: 使用即开发工作流（关键约束）

drafts.title: vendor/ is strictly read-only — all modifications go to the port layer
drafts.compiledTruth: vendor/reference material is strictly read-only. No modifications to vendor/reference files are permitted — any needed change must be migrated to the owned port layer (extensions/ or skills/). With current vendor submodules retired, upstream reference is obtained by temporary clone outside the tracked repo when needed. The port layer must never import or source from vendor/ paths. This is a hard discipline enforced by code review and architecture constraint, not a guideline.

drafts.title: Unidirectional dependency: extensions to skills/prompts, never to vendor
drafts.compiledTruth: Dependencies flow strictly one way: extensions/ may call into skills/ and prompts/ (consuming content resources), but extensions/ must never depend on vendor/. Skills and prompts must not depend on any code at all. This enforces a clean separation where business logic (extensions) consumes declarative content resources (skills/prompts), while vendor remains an inert reference consulted only manually during porting and upgrades. The dependency graph has no cycles and no vendor-reaching edges.

drafts.title: UPSTREAM.md is a real-time maintained traceability document
drafts.compiledTruth: UPSTREAM.md must be maintained as the live traceability document for upstream/reference provenance. Retired references record upstream URL, last pinned SHA, retirement date, and on-demand clone/diff workflow. Every port-layer resource derived from upstream code must be traceable to its upstream source via UPSTREAM.md. Owned functionality (e.g., retry-stream-eof, memory architecture) is explicitly excluded. This keeps the provenance of every port-layer artifact auditable.

drafts.title: Vendor SHA bumps are standalone commits, always paired with adaptation commits
drafts.compiledTruth: Retired vendor references are not bumped in-tree. When upstream review is needed, clone the upstream temporarily outside the tracked repo, compare against the last pinned SHA recorded in UPSTREAM.md, and commit only owned port-layer adaptations. Re-adding a retired vendor submodule requires an explicit new decision.

drafts.title: Phase 1 does not extract shared/ packages — wait for rule of three
drafts.compiledTruth: Phase 1 does not extract shared/ packages, even when apparent duplication exists across vision-core, imagine-core, gbrain-cli, or agent-loop. Extraction waits for the rule of three: at least three real consumers must naturally emerge before factoring out shared primitives. This came as a strong opinion from claude-opus during the multi-model debate. Premature abstraction is worse than tolerated duplication — shared APIs designed for hypothetical future consumers tend to be wrong.
drafts.sourceHeading: 5. 第一阶段不抽 shared/ 包

drafts.title: Ratchet rule: tolerate duplication exactly once, then systematize
drafts.compiledTruth: The rule-of-three is concretized as a ratchet mechanism: when a utility gains a second caller, extract it to an internal shared location. When a vendor file is modified a second time, promote it to a formal patch file. When an ad-hoc retry pattern appears a second time, consolidate it into a unified client. The ratchet tolerates duplication exactly once — the first occurrence is fine, the second triggers systematization. This avoids both premature abstraction and unchecked duplication sprawl.
drafts.sourceHeading: 6. 棘轮规则（rule-of-three 的具体化）

drafts.title: pi-astack never creates .pensieve/ — sedimentation goes through the brain
drafts.compiledTruth: pi-astack must never create a .pensieve/ directory — the pensieve project has been retired. All memory sedimentation goes through the brain infrastructure, with sediment as the sole writer. The markdown snapshot fallback for offline is maintained separately and gitignored. This enforces the architectural boundary that pi-astack (the workflow repo) does not contain or manage memory storage — that responsibility lives entirely in the brain infrastructure external to the repo.

drafts.title: No mechanical vendor bump scripts; no memory_intent write channel
drafts.compiledTruth: Two things are explicitly rejected. First: vendor bump and diff assessment must not be mechanized through npm run scripts — upstream change semantics require LLM reading the diff followed by human discussion; automation would bypass the semantic judgment only a human+LLM collaboration can provide. Second: the memory_intent main-session write channel (proposed in v6, removed in v6.5) is rejected — sediment's agent_end context extraction is sufficient for identifying user write intent from full conversation context, and adding an intent tool would increase dual-path merge complexity without benefit.

drafts.title: YAGNI: explicitly deferred decisions on npm publish, shared packages, CI
drafts.compiledTruth: This ADR explicitly defers decisions on: publishing pi-astack to the npm registry, extracting packages/ shared primitives, implementing affected-only CI, and adding lint/shellcheck CI. None of these are rejected — they are simply not decided here. The governing principle is YAGNI: do not design infrastructure for hypothetical use cases. Each will be reconsidered when genuine demand materializes, not before.
drafts.sourceHeading: 不在本 ADR 决策的事（YAGNI）
